text.title: React Component Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Common structural mistakes that cause tight coupling, poor testability, or component bloat.

antipattern.description: Prop drilling: passing user through App → Layout → Sidebar → UserMenu via props when only UserMenu uses it.
antipattern.reason: Prop drilling couples every intermediate component to data it does not use. Adding or removing the prop requires touching every level. Use React Context for cross-cutting data (authentication state, theme, locale) that is needed deep in the tree.

antipattern.description: Giant components: a Dashboard component with 500 lines of JSX handling multiple concerns (stats, navigation, activity feed, sidebar).
antipattern.reason: Giant components are hard to test, review, and modify in isolation. Compose from smaller components (DashboardStats, RecentActivity, DashboardSidebar) so each part can be tested and replaced independently.

antipattern.description: Business logic mixed into components: OrderForm containing validation logic, API calls, error handling, and state updates inline in the submit handler.
antipattern.reason: Business logic in components cannot be tested without rendering. Extract to hooks (useOrderSubmission) or services. The component's job is to render UI and delegate interactions; it should not know how orders are validated or submitted.

antipattern.description: Overwriting index.ts barrel files instead of appending to them. Agents regenerate the entire index.ts rather than adding missing exports.
antipattern.reason: Overwriting a barrel file removes existing exports. Consumers of those exports get broken imports that pass TypeScript compilation (if the re-exported types still exist elsewhere) but fail at runtime. Always read the existing index.ts before writing to it.

antipattern.description: Using React Context for cross-feature communication rather than keeping it within a feature scope.
antipattern.reason: Context created in features/auth/ and consumed in features/dashboard/ re-introduces the coupling the feature boundary was designed to prevent. Cross-feature data belongs in a shared store, shared hook at the app level, or URL state.
